speaker: interviewer
text: Traub's interview. So we were on the train back to Sighet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The memory is very vivid of the trains coming from east full of the wounded soldiers. And I don't know what I saw then. I don't know whether I felt-- I couldn't have possibly felt sorry for-- for-- I-- for German uniforms. Because by that time, it was very clear that they were the Amalek, the enemy of the people. And I particularly remember one face-- blonde hair, face bandaged. And I don't think I had hatred in my heart, but I know that I didn't feel, oh, poor soldier. Because I remembered my brother, Moshe [NON-ENGLISH] telling what he told us about what he saw in-- in Russia-- in occupied Russia, I should say. And I knew that my brother, Alex, was still in-- in that brigade, and God knows what might happen to him even though he was still in Hungary. And as I said, by then there was something in the air which was terrifying, which was panicky, and it was Amalek. Amalek came to Hungary. And so the memory of those soldiers, wounded soldiers, was not one of pity or sorrow, that much I know. And then when I got home the plunging into the abyss began. Because by that time, the law was unrestricted, unequivocally, anti-Jewish, anti-Semitic, anti, anti, anti-Jewish, all promulgated by the Hungarian government. And this is what it consisted of. First of all, they took away all businesses from Jews, no two ways about it. And I remember coming home and seeing my father-- I think we arrived back to Sighet during the day, and it was very strange for me to see my father in the middle of the afternoon being at home. They took away his business. Just from one day to another, he had to give the keys to his business. And they did the same thing with all the other businessmen. Eventually-- this is in March, end of March-- eventually they forbade professionals-- doctors, lawyers-- to practice. But what I remember with a great deal of sorrow is how my father, who was still a young man-- not to me in those days, but he was in his early 50s. At the most, he was 53, 54 years old-- that this tall man suddenly became humped.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, he just collapsed, because he had no-- I mean-- I remember seeing him in the morning getting dressed, and he was always so dapper with the monogrammed shirt and-- and his pants being-- the crease, you know? We use to-- we use to joke about it that the fly on my father's-- the crease of my father's pants would commit suicide if he--

speaker: interviewee
text: And this man-- and he was so fastidious, and he was just wonderful. He was still fastidious, but he was a broken man. And that broke my heart very much. My mother, who was a very strong woman, very strong woman-- even though the death of her oldest child had broken her, and-- and-- and she stayed away from music and from many things-- but she said it's all right. She was a great believer. She said, God will help us. We'll overcome this, too. And sort of Passover was approaching, and she said, well, God took us out from Egypt, from Israel. Things will be all right. But I do remember my father. He was just a broken man. Just a broken man. And I am afraid it was around that time that I said to him, your Hungarians, Daddy. Look what they did. And I don't know whether I'm repeating myself, but his answer to me was these people are not my Hungarians. They are not my Hungarians. That was his answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that something going on in the town as well? People who had been Hungarian versus Romanian? Was there a loyalty--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, but I would say that by and large, my father's generation, my parents' generation-- clearly, they were Austrian-Hungarians I mean, for them it was the emperor Franz Joseph and all that. And we grew up-- we were born during Romania. So I am sure-- I don't know whether they felt as patriotic as my father felt. I don't know about that. But I'm sure that the, you know, culture for them was Hungarian culture in which they grew up.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then the minute the Hungarian authorities, the Hungarian government took away the business-- I mean, the livelihood-- other laws were promulgated right away. Curfew, yellow stars. I remember the cook sewing those yellow stars on jackets and garments and whatnot. And what else? Yes, curfew and yellow stars.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every day it was a new order. Jews have to give so much gold. Jews-- all-- all radios are being confiscated. Any-- any cameras are being confiscated. I had a little Friedlander camera, which was confiscated.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the confiscation done? Now, the Hungarian police, accompanied by a Hungarian-- two Hungarian gendarmes-- they were very dapper looking with their plum-- plume helmeted shakos-- I mean, this is what the helmet was called, the shako. They barged in with a list, and they would say, Mr. Fischman, this is what you are-- this is what you are supposed to give us, because we know this is what you have.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this happened every other day. They took silver candlesticks, silver whatever they saw. We want this, and we want this painting, and we want this rug. And this went on practically every other day. And I'm sure it went on the same way with everybody else. Now an interesting thing happened around first week in April. Some-- we didn't know, but we learned that some German officers came to town. And there was some little tank somewhere in some place of Sighet, but I don't remember seeing it. The point is that these officers-- SS officers, as it turned out-- were billeted in the best homes in Sighet. And two young SS officers were billeted in my parents' house. And I remember when Passover [BACKGROUND NOISE] came, the dining room-- well, my parents' house was a big house, and it had two entrances. One entrance, which led through two rooms into the dining room, was separate from the main entrance. That part of the house was taken. Was-- It was in that part of the house that these two SS young officers were billeted.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say billeted, what does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: It meant that they lived there. They were put there. Now, as I said, it had two room and a dining room. One of the rooms, the first room, used to be my room, so there was a bed. That's-- and I'm sure-- I don't remember, but I'm sure that their orderly brought another bed or whatever. But there were two officers, and they occupied practically half of the house. [INAUDIBLE] And we were very fearful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to live with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. Terrible. My mother wouldn't let me go out. She would say to me, put on a kerchief. Clearly, now I understand. I was 17 years old, fairly young and fairly good looking. I don't even imagine-- I don't even understand how she could have imagined-- I mean, these were [BACKGROUND NOISE] Amalek. I mean, what-- why-- I mean, there was the connotation that they-- maybe she was thinking of my brother told her. It just comes to me right now. Maybe this is what she was trying to warn me, you know. Don't show yourself the way you are. But then on top of that, something worse happened to my family, and to, I think, 20 other Jewish families in town. [SIGHS] This preceded after this constant expropriation of gold and whatever. And they were asking for this, and they were asking for that, and they were taking it. And these were Hungarian gendarmes and Hungarian police.

speaker: interviewee
text: Suddenly, two gendarmes with the Hungarian policemen came into my parents' house, and they arrested my father. They took him. Why? We didn't know. Why? They wouldn't tell. What did my father do? They wouldn't tell. My father had always been law abiding, paying his taxes. Clearly, what did he do? He didn't do any contraband. He didn't play-- he wasn't a black marketeer. We didn't listen to forbidden radio channels. The free-- well, they didn't have Free Europe in those days. It was the English Channel that was broadcasting. We didn't listen. It was forbidden. We didn't buy underground newspapers. What did my father do? But they took him. Eventually-- it was terrible. I remember my mother-- I mean, what did he do? Why do they? Where do you take him? They wouldn't say. Cruel. Cruel. Eventually, a couple of days later, we learned that so were 20 other Jewish men taken. Who were those Jewish men? Leading businessman, leading members of the community, and that they are incarcerated in a-- some sort of-- in a synagogue which they transformed into a prison. But we still didn't know why.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the why was understood about a week later when the next orders came. You have 12 hours to pack, and we'll put you-- and the Jews will be put in a ghetto. And then it became clear that the reason these 21-- I believe there were 21-- Jewish known members of the Jewish community where imprisoned was that they be kept as hostages in case something happens in the ghetto. You see, we did not know about the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising, which happened just about a year before. But the Nazis knew, and this time they wanted to make sure that if anything happens, they have hostages. And nothing is going to happen while they deport the Sighet Jews to the ghetto. 12 hours later, early in the morning-- it was the week after Passover, so it must have been end of April, early May. End of April, I believe. And Sighet is a very beautiful town, and it was the time when the lilacs began to bloom. The lilac in my mother's garden was very special, because it was double petaled purple lilac. And you could see already the buds. And this was the morning when they rang, and again, the gendarmes-- and we were packed by then, mother and I and the cook. And across the street, my brother's wife, Rose, Roshe, Rose, and the two little babies, my nieces-- we had our valises. And in the street where we lived, there were few Jews. It was a mixed neighborhood, so there were about four Jewish families. And they were all packed, and the gendarmes came, and they took us into towards another street where there were some more Jews. They put us in the center of the street where the cars and horses were driven, and they started to force us to march to the ghetto. The ghetto was in the Jewish section of the town, which was on the other side of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your neighbors aware of what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Those very neighbors in whose courtyard I played in as a child-- those very neighbors who were guests at my mother's dining room table months before peeked through the windows and turned their faces. And as I look back now, strange that it pains me so much. That trek through the main street in the middle of the street, driven like cattle, with the valises, with the baggages, with my little nieces, with the little infant who was five, six weeks old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Being driven to that [NON-ENGLISH] where my father had a big business, where people would come to buy, would tip their hat and say, how do you do Mr. Fischman? And turn to my mother, and say how are you? [SPEAKING HUNGARIAN], which is a very respectful way to address a lady in Hungarian. How are you my lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: These very people who used to watch me bicycle to town and say, [NON-ENGLISH], you have the first girl's bicycle. Because they never saw a girl's bicycle without-- without a hand bar. These neighbors who used to pinch my cheek when I was a little girl, they stood and watched stony-faced as we were driven to this town with the Hungarian gendarme using two truncheons on old people who couldn't walk fast enough. It was so shameful, so humiliating. But I know now that the shame was theirs, not ours. But I didn't know it then. And so we got to the ghetto. Now, they had two ghettos in Sighet. It was the big ghetto and the small ghetto. My mother and I, and my sister-in-law, Rose, and her two babies [SIGHS] were in the big ghetto. And my brother, my older brother and his wife and his child who was about a year and a half old-- a little boy, Andicka-- they lived in another part of the town closer to the Jewish section. So around that part, they set-- they set up-- the Hungarians-- set up a second ghetto, which was called the small ghetto

speaker: interviewee
text: So we couldn't even get in touch with them. They wouldn't-- my brother couldn't even come to be with us. My father was incarcerated, and now we are in the ghetto. You were in the big ghetto, or the little--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the big ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm often asked, because I speak now and then, how did you live? What did you-- how could you buy food? I mean, were there barbed wires? No, there were not barbed wires, but what the Hungarians did-- and you have to understand that this was all done under the supervision and with the direction and under the control of the Nazis. But we still did not see any Nazis except the two German officers who where billeted in our house, who would leave in the morning and come back in the evening. And I'm sure that they were in the municipal building and-- and-- and writing out the orders and planning what was happening now. We were billeted in a small house in the big ghetto. And well, first of all, clearly we had money. And the way the ghetto was cut off from the rest of the city was not done the way it had been done in Warsaw and in the big ghettos. Because you see, in retrospect, we know they knew that this was going to be over a very short duration.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they didn't bother to erect walls or put up barbed wire. But every street that was designated as part of the ghetto had two policemen guarding it. Beginning of the street, end of the street. So you couldn't come in, and you couldn't go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: How we live-- but they did let in Hungarian farmer women who would come and sell their produce. Eggs, 10 times the price. Milk, 10 times the price. That's how we lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we didn't stay that long in the ghetto that food should have become a real problem. What happened meanwhile was that my father had some sort of a gallbladder attack in the prison, which we didn't know about. But a policeman took him to a doctor to give him some medication, a shot of something or other. In the process, my father somehow succeeded to bribe him by asking him, would you go and get someone from my family to come to visit me? Because within days, there came into the ghetto a young Hungarian man.

speaker: interviewee
text: He wasn't that young. I mean, today I call him young, but then he must have been a man in his 30s. I remember his trench coat, blonde with a mustache. And he was looking for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And again, we got very scared. Why is this detective looking for us? And he came, and he said that special provision-- I can take-- and he pointed to me-- I can take you to see your father for a brief visit. And my mother said, well, couldn't-- I would like to go. No, he said. If I walk with you, they may suspect. She's younger and so she-- people outside when I walk out from here, you know, they may not pay any attention.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took me to see my father. And that's when I learned that he was part of the hostage contingent. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I saw him once more, but he wanted to know how my mother was, and he wanted to know how the grandchildren were. And I said everybody was fine. And I said to him that during the night, we can hear the Russian cannons from across the mountains. Because the Russians-- you see, Sighet is surrounded by a range of the Carpathian mountains, which eventually borders, the southern the-- the eastern and southern part of Transylvania. And so we saw those mountains, and we heard the cannons, and we knew they were Russians. So I was telling my father, I said, you know, Dad. We used to pray that God, America, and England will help us, but now, it's God and the Russians. Because at night, the young people-- and I go with them. And we listened to the cannons, and they should be here any day. Any day. You did hear the cannon-- the sound of the cannons being fired. I was consoling him, and he was telling me that he has everything. No problem with food. They treat him respectfully. But he was a broken man. I saw that. But we parted, and I said-- and he said that he'll try to arrange it again with this nice detective who took me back. And I told my mother and my sister-in-law and the children that he's fine and everything will be all right. Next thing-- and this was early in May. No, no, around May 10 or 12, this very policeman walks into the ghetto-- now he knows exactly where we are with my father-- into the ghetto. And [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] the grandchildren were there. We couldn't get my brother, [NON-ENGLISH] because he was in the other ghetto. We couldn't get him. And he gave us an hour, and we were together-- my mother, my father. And then the detective said, we have to go, that my father has to go. And we stood, and he said so long to my mother. And they hugged and kissed. They loved each other very much, because my mother-- she fell in love with him when she was very young. And my father was not a very religious-- no, I shouldn't say very religious-- but my father was not a learned man in Talmud. He was a very secular man, and my uncle used to call him the song and dance man. The first lullaby I ever heard, I still weep when I hear that lullaby. It was my father who sang it to me. He was a very handsome man, a very gentle man, and a very kind man. And the story goes that my mother one nice day ran away from home, from one uncle to another. This was the extent of running away. And she left a note saying, if you don't let me marry Nandoor, you won't see me ever again. All this time she was at one of the uncles, and after that, clearly, they got married. So they loved each other very much. And they hugged and then kissed each other. I don't know what they said to each other. And then my father hugged the grandchildren and my sister-in-law, and I was the last one. And he was holding on to me, and the detective was tapping him on the shoulder. [SOBBING] He said, Mr. Fischman, we have to go. And he said, I cannot let go of my little girl. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're doing wonderfully. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He held me his arms, and he wouldn't let go. And the detective kept tapping him on the shoulders. And I said, Daddy, it's all right. I'll come and visit you in the jail. And after that, you know, the cannons across the mountains. It's all right, Daddy. And he was literally torn away from me. And it was the last time I saw him. He didn't know it would be the last time. I don't think he knew, but he sensed. Two days after that-- by this time, there was a Judenrat in the ghetto. They were Jewish policeman. And another order came two days later. The order was you have 12 hours to pack. The first four streets got only the order to pack, because they are liquidating the ghetto. We were the first of this group.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
